coredata.dc:title: Lithium phosphate incorporated carbon nanotube interlayer as an efficient polysulfide immobilizer for high performance lithium sulfur batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Dasarathan, Suriyakumar
Sung, Junghwan
Lee, You-Jin
Choi, Hae-Young
Park, Jun-Woo
Kim, Doohun
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries are the potential alternative for the lithium-ion batteries, owing to their remarkable energy density and specific capacity. Nevertheless, the lower utilization of active materials and the “shuttle effect” have impeded their widespread commercialization. To address these hurdles, a pioneering method has been proposed, involving a lithium phosphate-incorporated multi-walled carbon nanotube (Li3PO4@MWCNT) interlayer. The Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer primarily serves as a physical barrier against polysulfide shuttling. Its highly conductive cross-link structure enables it to adsorb chemically derived lithium polysulfides (LiPS) and catalyze their conversion by incorporating Li3PO4 into the MWCNT matrix. This synergistic effect of immobilizing and converting LiPS results in a significant reduction in the “shuttle effect,” leading to enhanced sulfur utilization. The experimental results evidence the enhanced performance of the Li-S cells, with a capacity of 381 mAh g−1 at 0.1C and high C-rate performance of 393 mAh g−1 at 1C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-S battery
MWCNT
Polysulfide immobilizer
Lithium phosphate
Catalytic effect
Phosphorous interlayer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FE-SEM image shows the cross-section of the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer (with a thickness of 30 µm). (b) A magnified view of the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer. (d) The EDS elemental mapping of the FE-SEM image of Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer. (e) XRD pattern of the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer, with the corresponding ICSD pattern. (f) Thermogravimetric analysis of MWCNT carried out in an Ar atmosphere.

(a) Working mechanism of the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer during charge-discharge process. (b) Cyclic voltammetry curves of Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer with S@MWCNT cathode at a scan rate of 0.1 mVs−1. (c) Polysulfide dissolution experiment for PE and Li3PO4@MWCNT coated separator observed after 24 hr.

(a) Cycling performance for Li3PO4@MWCNT and PE interlayer with corresponding columbic efficiency for 100 cycles. (b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles of the Li-S cells with Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer at 0.1C discharge rate. (c) Rate-performance value for different C-rates with Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer and PE separator. (d) Electrochemical impedance spectra after 100 cycles with the corresponding equivalent circuit.

head.ce:title: Lithium phosphate incorporated carbon nanotube interlayer as an efficient polysulfide immobilizer for high performance lithium sulfur batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Battery Research Division, Electrical Materials Research Division, Korea Electrotechnology Research Institute, Changwon 51543, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Battery Research Division, Electrical Materials Research Division, Korea Electrotechnology Research Institute, Changwon 51543, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electro-Functionality Materials Engineering, University of Science and Technology (UST), Daejeon 305-333, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electro-Functionality Materials Engineering, University of Science and Technology (UST), Daejeon 305-333, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Battery Research Division, Electrical Materials Research Division, Korea Electrotechnology Research Institute, Changwon 51543, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries are the potential alternative for the lithium-ion batteries, owing to their remarkable energy density and specific capacity. Nevertheless, the lower utilization of active materials and the “shuttle effect” have impeded their widespread commercialization. To address these hurdles, a pioneering method has been proposed, involving a lithium phosphate-incorporated multi-walled carbon nanotube (Li3PO4@MWCNT) interlayer. The Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer primarily serves as a physical barrier against polysulfide shuttling. Its highly conductive cross-link structure enables it to adsorb chemically derived lithium polysulfides (LiPS) and catalyze their conversion by incorporating Li3PO4 into the MWCNT matrix. This synergistic effect of immobilizing and converting LiPS results in a significant reduction in the “shuttle effect,” leading to enhanced sulfur utilization. The experimental results evidence the enhanced performance of the Li-S cells, with a capacity of 381 mAh g−1 at 0.1C and high C-rate performance of 393 mAh g−1 at 1C.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the preceding decade, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have consistently maintained their position as the leading energy storage systems, finding applications in a broad spectrum of electronic devices, including laptops and smartphones. However, as the years pass, traditional LIBs are gradually approaching their theoretical energy density limits, which presents a challenge in keeping up with the rapid advancements in electric vehicle technology [1,2].

In this context, lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries are gaining increasing attention due to their impressive specific capacity (1675 mA h g−1) and energy density (2600 Wh kg−1) derived from sulfur. Apart from their high energy density, sulfur offers cost-effectiveness and non-toxicity as it is abundant in nature, making these batteries environmentally friendly [3,4]. It appears that elemental sulfur is considered an ideal cathode material; however, there are several intrinsic characteristics of sulfur that hinder the commercialization of Li-S batteries [5,6]. Firstly, both elemental sulfur and its discharge product, lithium sulfide (Li2S), exhibit insulating properties, which result in low utilization of sulfur [7]; Secondly, a significant volume expansion occurs during the conversion from sulfur to Li2S due to the difference in their densities. This expansion could potentially damage the cathode structure [8,9]; Lastly, and of utmost significance, the lithium polysulfides (LIPS) that arise as discharge intermediates from sulfur can dissolve in the liquid electrolyte, traverse through the separator, and even react with the lithium anode. This constitutes the unavoidable detrimental “shuttle effect,” leading to ongoing and irreversible loss of active sulfur, resulting in capacity fading and low coulombic efficiency [9,10]. Currently, two state-of-the-art approaches have been proposed to address these issues [11].

Firstly, diverse sulfur hosts and cathode structures are developed to accommodate the significant volume expansion and to tightly bind sulfur within the cathode region [12–15]. Additionally, various functional interlayers and separators are designed to mitigate the “shuttle effect.” [16,17]. Due to its simpler configuration and arrangement, this latter approach is a significant source of inspiration for researchers in that regard. The concept of functional interlayers was first introduced by Manthiram et al. In their work, a conducting carbon interlayer is sandwiched between the sulfur cathode and separator, resulting in a significant enhancement of the electrochemical performance of Li-S batteries [18]. Subsequently, a variety of materials are employed to create freestanding interlayers or functional coatings on the commercial separator. Initially, carbon materials are directly utilized for these functional separators due to their high conductivity, which can facilitate sulfur utilization and lead to high discharge capacity [19]. For example, nanostructured carbon-based materials barely suppress the “shuttle effect” through weak physical obstruction, resulting in poor cycling stability during extended cycling processes. Therefore, heteroatom-doped carbon materials play a crucial role in interlayers for Li-S batteries by chemically adsorbing active sulfur, enhancing electron transport, and facilitating lithium-ion diffusion. This approach effectively mitigates the “shuttle effect,” leading to improved battery performance. The chemically functionalized carbon interlayer played a pivotal role in inhibiting the diffusion of Li2Sx towards the anode side. This effect was achieved through a dual capturing mechanism involving both physical trapping and a robust interaction between Li2Sx and elemental P [20].

In the current study, we employed a straightforward approach, casting a Li3PO4@MWCNT composite into a PE separator as a polysulfide immobilizer in Li-S batteries. The Li3PO4 coating facilitates rapid Li-ion diffusion during the charge/discharge process, leading to high ionic conductivity at higher C-rates and reducing cell resistance [29,30]. This approach resulted in a discharge capacity of 381 mAh g−1 even after 100 cycles at 0.1C.

The Li3PO4-incorporated MWCNT carbon composite (Li3PO4@MWCNT) was fabricated by mechanical mixing, with a weight percent ratio of 7:1.5:1:0.5 wt% Li3PO4: MWCNT: Super-P: PVDF, which was dispersed in NMP solvent from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. The Li3PO4@MWCNT slurry was cast onto a PE membrane (pore volume: 43%, pore size: 60 nm, thickness: 16 µm, W-scope, Tokyo, Japan) to achieve a film thickness of 24 µm. Subsequently, the composite was vacuum-dried at 55 °C for 12 h.

Sulfur powder (Sigma-Aldrich, <99%, USA) was mixed with multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, diameter = 9.5 nm, Nanocyl, Belgium) in ethanol using a ball-milling process (450 rpm, 30 min, 5 cycles) at a weight ratio of S:MWCNT = 7:3 wt%. After obtaining the composite cathode material, any remaining traces of solvents were removed in a vacuum oven by treating it at 70 °C for 24 h and then heating it at 155 °C for 2 h to ensure proper elemental S diffusion. The MWCNT-based cathode was prepared with a controlled film thickness of 200 µm on an Al current collector, having a mass loading of 1.32 mg cm−2, and comprising the active material: conducting agent: binder in a ratio of 70:20:10, with S/MWCNT: Super-P:CMC (3 wt%), SBR (48 wt%), serving as the cathode material. A 24 µm thick Li3PO4@MWCNT layer was coated on a PE separator, serving as a supporting film, and attached with the Li3PO4@MWCNT-coated surface facing towards the cathode part. A 500 µm thick Li-metal foil was used as the anode. The electrolyte consisted of 1 M lithium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.0 %) dissolved in a mixture of DOL and DME in a 1:1 (v:v) ratio, with a 1 wt% LiNO3 (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.99 %) additive. The coin cell was subjected to galvanostatic charging/discharging at a C-rate of 0.1C. Rate performances were evaluated using a multichannel battery tester (MACCORE) for various C-rates ranging from 0.1C to 1C, within a potential range of 1.8–2.8 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using an electrochemical setup (Bio Logic model, VMP3, France). CV was conducted at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 within a voltage range of 1.6–3.0 V, while EIS was carried out after 100 cycles at different bias potentials, spanning a frequency range of 10 kHz to 100 MHz with an AC signal amplitude of 10 mV.The dissolution of Li2S8 (0.01 M) in 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) was studied, comparing the PE separator (W-scope, pore volume: 43%, pore size: 60 nm, thickness: 16 µm, Tokyo, Japan) and the Li3PO4@MWCNT as a polysulfide immobilizer in an H-cell configuration.

Morphological characterization of the samples was conducted using a Hitachi S4800 field-emission scanning electron microscope. Cross-sectional images were obtained from the scissor-cut Li3PO4@MWCNT-coated PE separator. X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) was performed utilizing a Philips X’pert-MPD X-ray diffractometer equipped with a PANalytical X’celerator detector, employing graphite monochromatized Cu Kᾳ radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å).

The Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer, fabricated using a slurry casting process, was subjected to FE-SEM analysis. Fig. 1 (a) showcases the cross-sectional image of the scissor-cut interlayer, while Fig. 1(b) presents the cross-linked framework of the Li3PO4@MWCNT composite, emphasizing the presence of Li3PO4 particles in the powder form. The powder comprises coarse and irregular-shaped particles, while the conductive Super-P of approximately 100 nm size represents the spherical particles. The carbon nanotube (CNT), which may appear as a slender and elongated cylindrical structure, has a diameter ranging from a few nanometers to tens of nanometers, while its length may extend up to several micrometers. Fig. 1(c) and 1(d) depict the EDS analysis and corresponding elemental mapping images of the Li3PO4@MWCNT composite from the selected part (rectangular dotted red line) of Fig. 1(a), respectively. The conductive material (CNT and Super P) contributes to the weight composition of carbon, while F- is detected from Pvdf. Li, P, O are detected from Li3PO4. The amount of phosphorous is used to calculate the weight percent of Li. The corresponding elemental mapping results demonstrate the uniform distribution of C, O, F, and P elements throughout the layer, as shown in Fig. 1(d). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to characterize the Li3PO4@MWCNT diffraction peaks, as depicted in Fig. 1(e). The diffraction peaks match the ICSD pattern, as confirmed from the ICSD 98-001-0257 reference. Fig. 1(f) illustrates the results of thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA), which shows the decomposition rate of MWCNT under an Ar atmosphere. This indicates the thermal stability of MWCNT, which has a decomposition rate of 4 wt% at 800 °C, while PE decomposes entirely at 450 °C [31,33]. Hence, it can be deduced that the Li3PO4@MWCNT coated polyethylene demonstrates superior thermal stability and enhanced electrical conductivity of the interlayer in comparison with the standard PE.

In Fig. 2 (a), the electrode configuration with an electrolyte containing LiFTSI salt and LiNO3 as an additive in DOL/DME is illustrated. The higher polarization current signifies rapid reactions, directly correlating with the electrocatalytic behavior of Li3PO4@MWCNT under experimental conditions [21]. Fig. 2(b) shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve of the Li-S cells with the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer, tested at a stable scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 successively. The CV curve of the Li-S cells manifests two cathodic peaks at about 2.30 and 2.05 V, which can be assigned to the reduction from elemental sulfur to soluble long-chain LiPS (lithium polysulfides) and finally to insoluble short-chain Li2S2/Li2S. The corresponding anodic peaks are due to the converse transformation from Li2S2/Li2S to elemental sulfur [22,23]. It is noteworthy that the CV curve of the Li-S cell with the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer demonstrates the largest peak current and the best multicycle consistency in the cathodic peaks. This manifestation indicates faster redox reaction kinetics and better reversibility of the Li-S cell with the Li3PO4 interlayer [24]. The improved reversibility can be attributed to the reduction in the “shuttle effect” and lower loss of active sulfur, due to the chemical adsorption of LIPS by the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer, as previously verified (Fig. 2(b)). The acceleration in redox reaction kinetics can also be attributed to the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer, which facilitates lithium-ion diffusion, as indicated by the EIS measurements [25]. In Fig. 2(c), a polysulfide adsorption test was conducted using 0.01 M Li2S8 in a mixture of 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME), with a volume ratio of 1:1, to analyze the chemical adsorption of LiPSs. The mixture was placed on the right side of the cell, and a pure solvent mixture was equilibrated for 24 h. The Li3PO4@MWCNT was sandwiched between two cells, along with an identical PE separator, with Li3PO4@MWCNT on the right side, facing the polysulfide solution. After 24 h, the pristine PE separator noticeably changed its color to reddish-brown due to polysulfide dissolution. Conversely, the Li3PO4@MWCNT composite interlayer exhibited no significant alteration in color, thereby denoting the successful suppression of polysulfides. This outcome can be ascribed to the synergistic effect of the physical capture furnished by the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer, in addition to the Li3PO4 coating on the surface of MWCNT, which also contributed to the physical adsorption of the Li2Sx. The superior performance in adsorbing a greater amount of Li2Sx from the sulfur cathode showcases the robust chemical interaction between the discharged polysulfides of Li2Sx and the Li3PO4@MWCNT.

Fig. 3 (a) displays the cycling performances at 0.1C of the Li-S cells with the PE separator and Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer. The Li-S cell with the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer delivers an initial discharge capacity of 945 mA h g−1, implying high sulfur utilization. In contrast, the Li-S cells with the PE separator initiate with a discharge capacity of 845 mA h g−1, corresponding to lower sulfur utilization. After 100 cycles, the Li-S cell with the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer maintains a discharge capacity of 376 mAh g−1 with a coulombic efficiency of 99 %. The discharge capacities of the Li-S cells with the PE separator decay to 230 mAh g−1, much lower than that of the Li3PO4 interlayer. The corresponding initial charge-discharge profiles are shown in Fig. 3b. It is worth noting that the charge-discharge profiles of the Li-S cell with the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer exhibit the smallest potential hysteresis, indicating lower electrochemical polarization [26]. Owing to the effective immobilization of LIPS through chemical adsorption, the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer efficiently curbs the shuttle effect, resulting in reduced loss of active sulfur within the Li-S cell. Following 100 cycles, the cell maintains a discharge capacity of 381 mA h g−1, indicating an exceptionally low-capacity fading rate per cycle. Fig. 3c illustrates the rate capabilities of the three types of Li-S cells. Across all current densities ranging from 0.1 to 1C, the Li-S cell featuring the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer exhibits consistently higher discharge capacities. Specifically, it attains discharge capacities of 861, 490, 459, 393, and 620 mA h g−1 at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1C, and 0.1C, respectively. In contrast, the Li-S cell using the PE separator displays the poorest rate capability, delivering mere capacities of 611, 451, 377, 336, and 509 mA h g−1 across the same range of current densities. The lower polarization potential further verifies the accelerated redox reaction kinetics within the Li-S cell containing the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer [4,26]. Fig. 3d depicts the outcomes of EIS evaluations carried out on the Li-S cells. The Li-S cells having the Li3PO4 interlayer manifest a lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) compared to those having the PE separator. The depressed semicircle can be noticed at the highest frequencies, which corresponds to the parallel combination of surface film capacitance Cf and surface film resistance Rf. Additionally, the thin surface film resistance Rs is originate from the dissolved polysulfides Li2Sx that happened during charge-discharge process [32]. This suggests enhanced conductivity and facilitated charge transfer attributed to the presence of the Li3PO4 interlayer [27]. Capitalizing on its catalytic effect, heightened conductivity, and facilitated charge transfer, the Li-S cell incorporating the Li3PO4 interlayer achieves an exceptional rate capability, boasting an astonishing discharge capacity of 393 mA h g−1 at 1C. In contrast, Li-S cells utilizing the PE separator only manage to deliver 336 mAh g−1. Due to mitigated “shuttle effect,” of lithium film within in the Li-S cell equipped with Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer maintains a more intact and smoother surface, exhibiting shallower cracks [26,27,28]. The Li3PO4 carbon composites were formulated into a paste and applied onto commercial separators using a cost-effective and scalable solution-casting process. The integration of Li3PO4 carbon composite interlayers played a pivotal role in alleviating the Li2Sx shuttle effect, thereby ensuring the reliability of Li–S cells characterized by outstanding rate capability and stable charge/discharge cycling performance. The Li3PO4@MWCNT carbon interlayers demonstrated effective immobilization effects, effectively impeding the dissolution of negatively charged chemical species (Li2Sx) present in the organic electrolyte. Moreover, the P-doping treatment applied to the hierarchical pore structure of various carbon materials added significant chemical interactions beyond serving solely as physical traps in the micropores. This treatment was instrumental in preventing the diffusion of Li2Sx. The Li3PO4 coating notably reduced cell resistance by expediting the transport of Li+ ions, thereby facilitating rapid redox kinetics throughout charge/discharge electrochemical reactions [29,30]. Enhancing the morphological attributes of a fibrous S cathode, the Li3PO4@MWCNT framework, in conjunction with Li3PO4 particle composites coated within the interlayer, enhanced the wettability of liquid electrolytes, thereby promoting improved ion transport. This heightened wettability led to a reduced E/S ratio, as both the fibrous cathodes and Li3PO4@MWCNT coated interlayer absorbed the electrolyte more efficiently and rapidly. It's important to note that excessive electrolyte usage may trigger side reactions and gas generation during the discharge/charge cycles of flexible Li–S batteries, posing a significant environmental risk. Thus, the use of an optimal amount of electrolyte not only enhances safety but also ensures stable electrochemical performance [30,34]. Furthermore, the thermal safety of the Li-S battery was positively impacted by the Li3PO4@MWCNT coated interlayer, as evidenced by TGA analysis revealing the improved thermal decomposition rate of MWCNT. This enhancement indicates that Li3PO4@MWCNT will indicates superior performance even under high-temperature operating conditions [33].

In conclusion, we have successfully demonstrated a straightforward approach to achieve high-performance Li-S batteries. The composite interlayer of Li3PO4@MWCNT serves a dual purpose by acting as a physical barrier against LIPS due to its excellent conductivity and cross-linking structure. Additionally, it offers chemical adsorption of LIPS and catalyzes their conversion, facilitated by the incorporation of Li3PO4. The combined effect of Li3PO4 and MWCNT in terms of physical obstruction, chemical adsorption, and catalytic conversion yields a substantial reduction in the “shuttle effect,” leading to enhanced discharge capacity, exceptional cycling stability, and elevated rate capability. Consequently, the Li-S cell employing the Li3PO4 interlayer achieves a high discharge capacity of 945 mAh g−1, maintaining 381 mAh g−1 even after the 100 th cycle at 0.1C. These results underscore its commendable cycling and rate performance. The affordability and remarkable electrochemical performance of the Li3PO4@MWCNT interlayer render it highly suitable for commercial application in Li-S batteries.

Suriyakumar Dasarathan: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Junghwan Sung: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. You-Jin Lee: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Hae-Young Choi: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Jun-Woo Park: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Doohun Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

Doohun Kim reports a relationship with Korea Electrotechnology Research Institute that includes: employment.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new approach to improve cycle performance of rechargeable lithium-sulfur batteries by inserting a free-standing MWCNT interlayer
